questions.question: Who is eligible to use Custom Agents in Notion?

options.text: Individual users on the Free or Personal Plan
options.explanation: Custom Agents are currently only available to users on Notion’s Business or Enterprise Plan.

options.text: Teams on the Business or Enterprise Plan
options.explanation: This is correct. Custom Agents are designed for Business and Enterprise workspaces to automate recurring workflows.

options.text: Workspace Admins only
options.explanation: While Admins have specific control settings, anyone on the eligible plan can create and use Custom Agents.

options.text: External contractors
options.explanation: Eligibility is based on the Notion plan the user is on, not their external role.

questions.question: How does a Custom Agent differ from the Notion Agent (the assistant in the bottom right corner)?

options.text: Notion Agent runs autonomously in the background on triggers.
options.explanation: Notion Agent is on-demand, whereas Custom Agents are designed to run autonomously in the background.

options.text: Custom Agents are on-demand, whereas Notion Agent runs autonomously.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The Notion Agent is on-demand, while Custom Agents run autonomously via triggers.

options.text: Custom Agents are designed for one-off tasks.
options.explanation: Notion Agent handles one-off tasks, while Custom Agents handle recurring, automated workflows.

options.text: Notion Agent uses precise instructions, while Custom Agents use natural language.
options.explanation: Custom Agents use precise instructions, whereas Notion Agent helps draft and summarize content.

questions.question: Where do you configure the specific pages and databases an Agent should use as context?

options.text: In the Instructions field when you create the agent.
options.explanation: Linking pages in the Instructions field does not grant access; you must use the 'Tools and access' section.

options.text: In the Triggers section of Settings.
options.explanation: Triggers determine *when* the agent runs, not *what* data it can access.

options.text: In the Tools and access section of Settings.
options.explanation: This is correct. The 'Tools and access' section is where you explicitly grant the agent permission to view specific pages and databases.

options.text: In the Model section of Settings.
options.explanation: The Model section is for selecting the AI model (e.g., GPT, Claude), not for configuring access.

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid Notion trigger for a Custom Agent?

options.text: A message posted to a private Slack channel.
options.explanation: Private Slack channels are not supported for Custom Agent triggers; only public channels are supported.

options.text: A page being added to a database.
options.explanation: This is a valid trigger. Agents can run when pages are added, updated, removed, or when comments are added.

options.text: A user clicking a link in a Notion page.
options.explanation: Notion triggers are specific to database events, comments, and mentions; they do not react to link clicks.

options.text: A user logging into the workspace.
options.explanation: Login events are not listed as supported triggers for Custom Agents.

questions.question: How can you allow a Custom Agent to browse the internet to retrieve external information?

options.text: Enable it in the Triggers section.
options.explanation: Web access is a toggle setting, not a trigger configuration.

options.text: Turn on the Web access toggle in Settings.
options.explanation: This is correct. You find the 'Web access' toggle in the Settings panel of the agent.

options.text: Connect it via the Slack integration settings.
options.explanation: Slack integration handles communication, not web browsing.

options.text: It is enabled by default for all agents.
options.explanation: Web access is optional and must be explicitly toggled on or off.

questions.question: Before setting up a Slack trigger for a Custom Agent, what administrative step is required?

options.text: The agent creator must have Full Access permissions.
options.explanation: Access permissions are for the agent itself, not the specific admin task required to enable the integration.

options.text: A Slack workspace admin must enable the Notion integration for Custom Agents.
options.explanation: This is correct. An admin must enable the Notion integration to give the agent the necessary read/write permissions in Slack.

options.text: The agent must be shared with the entire workspace.
options.explanation: Sharing the agent is a separate step that happens after the integration is enabled.

options.text: The agent must be connected to the Slack AI connector.
options.explanation: The Slack AI connector is required for general Slack AI features, but the specific Notion integration for Custom Agents must be enabled by an admin first.

questions.question: What happens if you want to debug a run that failed unexpectedly?

options.text: Open the Chat tab and ask the agent to explain itself.
options.explanation: The Chat tab is for testing instructions and one-off tasks, not for debugging historical failed runs.

options.text: Open the Activity tab to see the trigger, actions taken, and errors.
options.explanation: This is correct. The Activity tab provides logs showing what triggered the run, what the agent did, and any error messages.

options.text: Check the Version history to see if a change caused the error.
options.explanation: While Version history is useful for reverting changes, the Activity tab is the primary tool for debugging a specific failed run.

options.text: Contact Notion Support immediately.
options.explanation: Users should use the Activity tab and Logs to debug first before contacting support.

questions.question: What is the correct process to restore a previous version of a Custom Agent configuration?

options.text: Re-create the agent from a template.
options.explanation: Templates are for starting new agents, not restoring old configurations.

options.text: Access Version history on the Agent page and select a version to restore.
options.explanation: This is correct. You navigate to the Agent page, access Version history, review past versions, and select one to revert to.

options.text: Edit the instructions and manually re-enter the previous steps.
options.explanation: Version history allows for an exact restoration without manual re-entry.

options.text: Contact an Admin to revert the changes.
options.explanation: Users can restore versions themselves; Admins are not required for this specific action.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between 'Can View and Interact' and 'Full Access' permissions?

options.text: Can View and Interact users can edit instructions, but Full Access users cannot.
options.explanation: Both groups can view instructions; Full Access allows editing configurations and managing access.

options.text: Full Access users can view activity logs, but Can View and Interact users cannot.
options.explanation: Both can view activity logs; Full Access users have more management capabilities.

options.text: Can View and Interact users can run and chat with the agent, but Full Access users can also manage access.
options.explanation: This is correct. 'Can View and Interact' allows running and chatting, while 'Full Access' includes the ability to manage access, triggers, and models.

options.text: Can View and Interact users can edit triggers, but Full Access users cannot.
options.explanation: Neither group can edit triggers; only Full Access can.

questions.question: Which models are supported for running a Custom Agent?

options.text: Only GPT-4.
options.explanation: Custom Agents support multiple models, including Claude, GPT, and Gemini.

options.text: Claude, GPT, and Gemini.
options.explanation: This is correct. Supported models include Claude, GPT, and Gemini, with 'Auto' as the recommended default.

options.text: Notion's proprietary model only.
options.explanation: Custom Agents are not limited to a proprietary model; they can use standard LLMs like GPT and Claude.

options.text: Auto is the only supported model.
options.explanation: While Auto is the recommended default, specific models can be selected if consistency is desired.

questions.question: When setting up a Slack trigger, which type of channel can the agent monitor?

options.text: All channels in the workspace.
options.explanation: Agents can only monitor public channels, not private ones.

options.text: Only channels where the agent is a member.
options.explanation: This is not a restriction; the agent can monitor public channels regardless of membership status in Notion.

options.text: Only channels with more than 10 members.
options.explanation: Channel size is not a factor; accessibility (public vs private) is the deciding factor.

options.text: Public channels.
options.explanation: This is correct. Custom Agents can only watch for events in public Slack channels.
